articles.title: You Can Go Inside a Volcano! (Seriously)
articles.summary: Imagine climbing into a volcano that's like a giant colorful cave. In Iceland, there's a volcano called Thríhnúkagígur where you can do just that! It's the only place on Earth where people can go inside a magma chamber—the room where hot, liquid rock used to be. The volcano hasn't erupted for 4,500 years, and its magma chamber is empty. Scientists think the magma got sucked back down into the ground. Visitors take an open elevator 700 feet down to explore the colorful walls, which are painted by minerals and tiny microbes. The chamber is bigger than two Statues of Liberty stacked on top of each other! Cool, right?

articles.title: A Giant Dam Between Alaska and Russia? Scientists Say It Could Save a Key Ocean Current
articles.summary: What if building a giant dam between Alaska and Russia could help save a crucial ocean current? Scientists are studying this idea! The current, called AMOC, is like a giant conveyor belt that moves warm water north and cold water south. It helps keep Europe warm and supports marine life. But global warming is weakening it, and if it collapses, Europe could get much colder, and sea levels could rise. The dam would block the Bering Strait, which might help the current. But it could also harm wildlife and Indigenous people. And it might not even work in all cases. It's a risky idea!

articles.title: The Baby-Making Contest That Divided Canada During the Great Depression
articles.summary: Imagine a contest where the mom with the most babies wins a huge fortune! That's exactly what happened in Canada during the Great Depression. A rich, eccentric lawyer named Charles Vance Millar died in 1926 and left a strange will. He promised a big chunk of his money to the Toronto mother who gave birth to the most children in ten years. The prize was worth about 9 million Canadian dollars today! The contest was called the Great Stork Derby. It sparked a media frenzy and divided the country. Some people thought it was funny, but others argued about morality and class. The contest lasted until 1936.
